perturbation.original_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of California, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement shall be governed and construed in accordance with the laws of the State of Delaware, without regard to the conflicts of law principles thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Changing the governing law to Delaware could create conflicts if aspects of the agreement violate California-specific labor laws, as California has unique employee protections.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Labor Code § 201 mandates specific deadlines for final wage payments to terminated employees. If the contract involves employees in California, changing the governing law to Delaware could lead to non-compliance with these requirements, as Delaware law might allow for different payment timelines that are less favorable to the employee.

EMPLOYMENT REGULATION AND SUPERVISION [200 - 2699.8] ( Division 2 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) PART 1. COMPENSATION [200 - 452] ( Part 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) CHAPTER 1. Payment of Wages [200 - 273] ( Chapter 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) ARTICLE 1. General Occupations [200 - 244] ( Article 1 enacted by Stats. 1937, Ch. 90. ) 201. (a) If an employer discharges an employee, the wages earned and unpaid at the time of discharge are due and payable immediately. An employer who lays off a group of employees by reason of the termination of seasonal employment in the curing, canning, or drying of any variety of perishable fruit, fish, or vegetables, shall be deemed to have made immediate payment when the wages of said employees are paid within a reasonable time as necessary for computation and payment thereof; provided, however, that the reasonable time shall not exceed 72 hours, and further provided that payment shall be made by mail to any employee who so requests and designates a mailing address therefor. (b) Notwithstanding any other law, the state employer shall be deemed to have made an immediate payment of wages under this section for any unused or accumulated vacation, annual leave, holiday leave, or time off to which the emp
perturbation.justification: The modified text changes the governing law from California to Delaware. The Law Explanation highlights that California Labor Code § 201 mandates specific deadlines for final wage payments to terminated employees. Scraped Law Snippet 1 provides the exact text of California Labor Code § 201, stating that if an employer discharges an employee, wages are due and payable immediately. By changing the governing law, the contract attempts to circumvent California's specific labor protections, potentially leading to non-compliance with its wage payment requirements, thereby creating a legal contradiction as the new governing law might not offer the same employee protections.

perturbation.original_text: Upon the Company's request, you shall promptly deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials and any other written materials without retaining, in whole or in part, any copies, extracts or other reproductions (whatever the form or storage medium) of such materials, and shall certify the destruction of such materials in writing to the Company.
perturbation.changed_text: Upon the Company's request, you shall deliver to the Company or destroy all written Evaluation Materials within 24 hours of the request. You may not retain any copies. You will certify the destruction of such materials in writing to the Company.
perturbation.explanation: Requiring destruction within 24 hours may contradict legal hold obligations if the materials are relevant to pending or potential litigation.
perturbation.law_explanation: FRCP 37(e) addresses the failure to preserve electronically stored information in anticipation or conduct of litigation. If the Evaluation Materials are relevant to a legal hold, a 24-hour destruction requirement would violate the duty to preserve evidence, potentially leading to sanctions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: Rule 37. Failure to Make Disclosures or to Cooperate in Discovery; Sanctions | Federal Rules of Civil Procedure | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you Rule 37. Failure to Make Disclosures or to Cooperate in Discovery; Sanctions (a) Motion for an Order Compelling Disclosure or Discovery. (1) In General. On notice to other parties and all affected persons, a party may move for an order compelling disclosure or discovery. The motion must include a certification that the movant has in good faith conferred or attempted to confer with the person or party failing to make disclosure or discovery in an effort to obtain it without court action. (2) Appropriate Court. A motion for an order to a party must be made in the court where the action is pending. A motion for an order to a nonparty must be made in the court where the discovery is or will be taken. (3) Specific Motions. (A) To Compel Disclosure. If a party fails to make a disclosure required by Rule 26(a) , any other party may move to compel disclosure and for appropriate sanctions. (B) To Compel a Discovery Response. A party seeking discovery may move for an order compelling an answer, designation, production, or inspection. This motion may be made if: (i) a deponent fails to answer a question asked under Rule 30 or 31 ; (ii) a corporation or other entity fails to make a designation under Rule 30(b)(6) or 31(a)(4) ; (iii) a party fails to answer an interrogatory submitted under Rule 33 ; or (iv) a party fails to produce documents or fails to respond that inspection will be permitted—or fails to permit inspection—as requested under Rule 34 . (C) Related to a Deposition. When taking an oral deposition, the party asking a question may complete or adjourn the examination before moving for an order. (4) Evasive or Incomplete Disclosure, Answer, or Response. For purposes of this subdivision (a), an evasive or incomplete disclosure, answer, or response must be treated as a failur

Secure .gov websites use HTTPS A lock ( Lock Locked padlock icon ) or https:// means you’ve safely connected to the .gov website. Share sensitive information only on official, secure websites. Federal Rules of Civil Procedure The Federal Rules of Civil Procedure govern civil proceedings in the United States district courts. The purpose of the Federal Rules of Civil Procedure is "to secure the just, speedy, and inexpensive determination of every action and proceeding." Fed. R. Civ. P. 1. The rules were first adopted by order of the Supreme Court on December 20, 1937, transmitted to Congress on January 3, 1938, and effective September 16, 1938. The Civil Rules were last amended in 2024. perturbation.justification: The modified text creates a contradiction because it mandates destruction of materials within 24 hours, which could violate legal hold obligations if those materials are relevant to pending or potential litigation. The Law Explanation states that this 24-hour requirement could violate the duty to preserve evidence, potentially leading to sanctions under FRCP 37(e). While the provided snippets don't explicitly detail legal hold requirements or sanctions for spoliation, Snippet 1 refers to Rule 37, which concerns 'Failure to Make Disclosures or to Cooperate in Discovery; Sanctions.' The imposition of such a short timeframe directly undermines the broader legal framework designed to ensure evidence preservation during litigation, as highlighted by the Perturbation and Law Explanations.

perturbation.original_text: You will be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by your representatives.
perturbation.changed_text: You will be responsible for any breach of this Agreement by your representatives, even if you took reasonable steps to prevent the breach.
perturbation.explanation: Holding someone liable regardless of their preventive actions may conflict with agency law principles and due process, especially if the representative acted outside the scope of their authority.
perturbation.law_explanation: The Restatement (Third) of Agency § 7.03 outlines the circumstances under which a principal is liable for the actions of an agent. Holding the principal responsible even when reasonable preventative steps were taken potentially broadens liability beyond what is legally permissible, especially if the agent's actions were not authorized or ratified.

perturbation.justification: The modification introduces language that holds a party responsible even when reasonable steps were taken to prevent a breach, potentially contradicting agency law principles as outlined in the Law Explanation, which references Restatement (Third) of Agency § 7.03. While the provided scraped snippets are not directly relevant or helpful, the Law Explanation suggests the altered contract broadens liability beyond what is legally permissible, because it could potentially include liability for unauthorized actions of agents. This contradicts standard legal expectations of liability.

perturbation.original_text: Accordingly, unless required by applicable law, including applicable securities laws that counsel has advised you require disclosure of Evaluation Materials, you agree that without the prior written consent of the Company, you will not, and you will direct your representatives not to, disclose to any person either the fact that you have been provided access to the Evaluation Materials or that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning a Possible Transaction between you and the Company as a result thereof, or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect to any such Possible Transaction, including the status thereof.
perturbation.changed_text: You agree that without the prior written consent of the Company, you will not, and you will direct your representatives not to, disclose to any person either the fact that you have been provided access to the Evaluation Materials or that discussions or negotiations are taking place concerning a Possible Transaction between you and the Company as a result thereof, or any of the terms, conditions or other facts with respect to any such Possible Transaction, including the status thereof.
perturbation.explanation: Removing the exception for disclosures required by applicable law, including securities laws, directly contradicts mandatory reporting obligations.
perturbation.contradicted_law: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5
perturbation.law_explanation: Securities Exchange Act of 1934, Section 10(b) and Rule 10b-5 prohibits making materially false and misleading statements or omissions in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. Removing the clause that allows disclosure when required by securities law makes it illegal to comply with SEC requirements, potentially resulting in significant fines and penalties.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. | Electronic Code of Federal Regulations (e-CFR) | US Law | LII / Legal Information Institute Please help us improve our site! × No thank you 17 CFR § 240.10b-5 - Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. CFR prev | next § 240.10b-5 Employment of manipulative and deceptive devices. It shall be unlawful for any person, directly or indirectly, by the use of any means or instrumentality of interstate commerce, or of the mails or of any facility of any national securities exchange , (a) To employ any device, scheme, or artifice to defraud, (b) To make any untrue statement of a material fact or to omit to state a material fact necessary in order to make the statements made, in the light of the circumstances under which they were made, not misleading, or (c) To engage in any act, practice, or course of business which operates or would operate as a fraud or deceit upon any person, in connection with the purchase or sale of any security . (Sec. 10; 48 Stat. 891 ; 15 U.S.C. 78j ) [ 13 FR 8183 , Dec. 22, 1948, as amended at 16 FR 7928 , Aug. 11, 1951] CFR Toolbox Law about... Articles from Wex Table of Popular Names Parallel Table of Authorities
perturbation.justification: The changed text removes a clause that allows disclosure when required by securities law. According to the Law Explanation, this makes it illegal to comply with SEC requirements. Scraped Law Snippet 1, 17 CFR § 240.10b-5, prohibits making untrue statements or omitting material facts in connection with the purchase or sale of any security. By removing the exception, the modified contract creates a scenario where compliance with the contract could lead to a violation of this law, because it may force a party to withhold legally mandated disclosures. Thus, the changed text creates a legal contradiction by requiring non-compliance with securities laws.

perturbation.original_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties hereto regarding the subject matter hereof.
perturbation.changed_text: This Agreement constitutes the entire agreement between the parties hereto regarding the subject matter hereof, except for any implied agreements or understandings the parties may have reached.
perturbation.explanation: Adding an exception for 'implied agreements or understandings' undermines the purpose of an integration clause and could lead to disputes over the enforceability of the contract.
perturbation.law_explanation: California Code of Civil Procedure § 1856, codifying the parol evidence rule, generally prevents the introduction of extrinsic evidence to contradict or vary the terms of a written agreement intended to be the final expression of the parties' agreement. By allowing for 'implied agreements or understandings,' the changed text contradicts this rule and could render the integration clause ineffective, making the contract more vulnerable to disputes based on pre-contractual discussions.
perturbation.scraped_snippet_1: California Code, CCP 1856 skip to content home accessibility FAQ feedback sitemap login x Quick Search: Bill Number Bill Keyword Home Bill Information California Law Publications Other Resources My Subscriptions My Favorites California Law >> >> Code Section Code Section Code: Select Code CONS BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Article: Section: Code: Select Code All BPC CIV CCP COM CORP EDC ELEC EVID FAM FIN FGC FAC GOV HNC HSC INS LAB MVC PEN PROB PCC PRC PUC RTC SHC UIC VEH WAT WIC Section: Keyword(s): Code Search Text Search Up^ << Previous Next >> cross-reference chaptered bills PDF | Add To My Favorites Search Phrase: Code Text Code of Civil Procedure - CCP PART 4. MISCELLANEOUS PROVISIONS [1855 - 2107] ( Heading of Part 4 amended by Stats. 1965, Ch. 299. ) TITLE 1. OF THE GENERAL PRINCIPLES OF EVIDENCE [1855 - 1866] ( Title 1 enacted 1872. ) 1856. (a) Terms set forth in a writing intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement. (b) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by evidence of consistent additional terms unless the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (c) The terms set forth in a writing described in subdivision (a) may be explained or supplemented by course of dealing or usage of trade or by course of performance. (d) The court shall determine whether the writing is intended by the parties as a final expression of their agreement with respect to the terms included therein and whether the writing is intended also as a complete and exclusive statement of the terms of the agreement. (e) Where a mistake or imperfection of the writing is put in issue by the pleadings, this section does not exclude evidence relevant to that issue. (f
perturbation.justification: The changed text introduces an exception for 'implied agreements or understandings,' which directly contradicts the parol evidence rule codified in California Code of Civil Procedure § 1856. The original text's integration clause aimed to prevent reliance on extrinsic evidence, while the changed text opens the door for such evidence by acknowledging 'implied agreements,' potentially undermining the contract's enforceability. Snippet 1 clearly states that terms in a final written agreement 'may not be contradicted by evidence of a prior agreement or of a contemporaneous oral agreement.' The modification weakens the integration clause, making the contract vulnerable to disputes based on pre-contractual discussions, and is therefore a direct contradiction.
